source.name: IRS SOI county statistics & migration data (California)
source.publisher: Internal Revenue Service, Statistics of Income

cadence: annual; SOI publishes county data ~2 years after the tax year
caveats: Federal (IRS) returns, not California tax data. Shown only as a demographic lens on the same taxpayer population — never combined with any California dollar figure.
Age is a single flag: primary taxpayer 60 or older. No finer age detail exists in any public tax data.
IRS counts slightly more filers than FTB (federal filers below California's filing threshold); the per-county correspondence ratio is published so the overlap is visible, not assumed.

Migration flows cover returns filed in consecutive years and matched by the IRS; small cells are suppressed at the source. The IRS enhanced its matching process starting with the 2022-23 edition, so that year is not perfectly comparable to earlier ones.
Migration is shown as a multi-year trend on purpose: any single year is a snapshot, and the pandemic-era peak would overstate the current pace.

data.framing: Federal (IRS) return statistics for the same Californians — shown as a demographic lens only, never combined with California tax figures.

statewide_totals.note: IRS counts slightly more filers than FTB — federal filers under California's filing threshold. Near-1 ratio is the evidence these tables describe the same people.

migration.trend_note: One year of migration is a snapshot, not a verdict — read the trend. The IRS enhanced its return-matching process starting with the 2022→23 edition, so the newest figure is not perfectly comparable to earlier years.
